<!-- prettier-ignore-start -->

# TS9005

**Category:** Typeclass Resolution · **Severity:** Error

## Message

```
summon requires a type argument: summon<Typeclass<Type>>()
```

## Explanation

summon&lt;T&gt;() resolves a typeclass instance at compile time.

Correct:
  summon&lt;Show&lt;Point&gt;&gt;()   — resolves Show instance for Point
  summon&lt;Eq&lt;User&gt;&gt;()      — resolves Eq instance for User

Incorrect:
  summon()               — missing type argument
  summon&lt;Point&gt;()        — Point is not a typeclass

<!-- prettier-ignore-end -->
<!-- generated:end -->

## Example

```typescript
import { summon } from "@typesugar/typeclass";
import type { Show } from "@typesugar/std";

interface Point {
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

/** @impl Show<Point> */
const showPoint: Show<Point> = {
  show: (p) => `(${p.x}, ${p.y})`,
};

const show = summon();
// error: TS9005 — summon requires a type argument: summon<Typeclass<Type>>()
```

## How to fix

`summon` resolves entirely at compile time from its type argument — there is no runtime value it could infer the instance from. Spell out the typeclass applied to the type:

```typescript
const show = summon<Show<Point>>();
show.show({ x: 1, y: 2 }); // "(1, 2)"
```

The instance must be in scope at the call site — defined locally (as above) or imported. Resolution is scope-based, not a global registry, so a `Show<Point>` instance in an unimported module will not be found.
